subject: History
overall_learning_objectives: Critically evaluate the historiographical limitations of Tawarikh as elite-centric narratives
Analyze the intersection of gender, capability, and patriarchal power through the reign of Raziyya Sultan
Investigate the structural shift from defensive Garrison towns to an aggressive imperial state
Deconstruct the 'Circle of Justice' as a socio-economic theory of medieval governance
Contrast the logistics and outcomes of Khalji and Tughluq responses to Mongol threats
Examine the 'Iqta' and 'Bandagan' systems as mechanisms of centralization and loyalty
Assess the legacy of Sher Shah Sur’s administrative model on subsequent imperial structures

lesson_plan.title: Urbanization and Political Centrality
topics_covered: Economic foundations: Dehliwal coinage and Jain merchant influence
The 'Seven Cities' of Delhi as a manifestation of dynastic legitimacy
Transition from Tomara regionalism to the Mamluk Sultanate
teaching_strategy: Spatial analysis: Why Delhi? (Geopolitical advantage of the Aravallis and Yamuna)
Discussion: The role of trade in propelling a city from commercial hub to imperial capital
Visual analysis of Dehliwal currency and its metallic composition
student_activities: Map the strategic locations of Siri, Tughluqabad, and Jahanpanah on a topographical map
Research the 'Delhi-i-Kuhna' and its significance as the nucleus of the Sultanate
assessment: Analytical response: 'How did commercial prosperity facilitate the political rise of Delhi?'
Chronological quiz on the Mamluk and Khalji transitions

lesson_plan.title: Historiography and the Elite Perspective
topics_covered: The sociology of Tawarikh: Authors as city-dwellers and court appendages
The 'Ideal Social Order': Birthright, Gender, and the 'Circle of Justice'
The exclusion of the subaltern in medieval Persian chronicles
teaching_strategy: Critical reading of Minhaj-i-Siraj: Identifying the theological basis for gender bias
Deconstructing the 'Circle of Justice' as a circular economic dependency
Inquiry: 'Whose history is missing from the Tawarikh?'
student_activities: Draft a 'Silent Voice' diary entry—how a peasant might view the 'Circle of Justice'
Critique the term 'Ideal Social Order' from a modern democratic perspective
assessment: Essay: 'The Tawarikh as a mirror of courtly anxieties rather than social reality'
Identification of the three primary limitations of Tawarikh authors

lesson_plan.title: The Raziyya Sultan Paradox
topics_covered: Meritocracy vs. Tradition: Raziyya’s capability as a threat to the nobility
Comparative Queenship: Rudramadevi (Disguise) vs. Didda (Influence) vs. Raziyya (Open Rule)
The role of the 'Forty' (Chahalgani) in undermining female sovereignty
teaching_strategy: Comparative analysis of epigraphical evidence: How Rudramadevi used titles to mask gender
Socratic debate: 'Was Raziyya’s failure administrative or purely social?'
Analyzing the 1240 CE coup as a reactionary move by the Turkish nobility
student_activities: Analyze Raziyya's coins and inscriptions to see how she asserted her identity
Write a comparative chart on the strategies used by medieval female rulers to gain legitimacy
assessment: HOTS: 'Why was Raziyya’s removal a victory for social order but a loss for meritocracy?'
Comparison between Minhaj-i-Siraj’s views and the actual capabilities of Raziyya

lesson_plan.title: Imperial Expansion and Frontiers
topics_covered: Internal Frontier: The colonization of the Doab and agricultural expansion
External Frontier: Southern campaigns of Malik Kafur (1310-1311)
Logistics of maintaining 'Garrison Towns' in hostile hinterlands
teaching_strategy: Mapping the 'Southern Trail': Tracing the Khalji army's route to Madurai
Discussion on the 'Extraction Economy': Capturing elephants, horses, and gold as state revenue
Analyzing the ecological transformation of the Ganga-Yamuna Doab
student_activities: Calculate the distance and logistical challenges of the Devagiri and Madurai expeditions
Define 'Hinterland' and explain why its control was vital for the survival of Garrison towns
assessment: Mapping assessment: Identification of key military nodes in the South
Short answer: 'Differentiate between the internal and external frontier policies'

lesson_plan.title: Mosques, Architecture, and State Piety
topics_covered: Quwwat al-Islam and the Alai Darwaza: Architectural synthesis
The mosque as a tool for political mobilization and 'Protector' status
The Delhi Sultanate's claim to the Caliphate through 'Qibla' and 'Khutba'
teaching_strategy: Architectural analysis: From corbelled arches to true arches and domes
Discussion: Why did Sultans build multiple mosques in a single capital area?
Investigating the 'Jahanpanah' concept (Sanctuary of the World)
student_activities: Sketch and label the evolution of the arch from the Qutb complex to the Tughluq era
Explain the political significance of the 'Khutba' mentioning the Sultan’s name
assessment: Analytical response: 'Architecture as a medium of expressing imperial and religious authority'
Identification of architectural terms: Sahn, Liwan, Maqsura

lesson_plan.title: Administrative Divergence: Khalji vs. Tughluq
topics_covered: The 'Iqta' System 2.0: Checking the power of Muqtis
Fiscal experiments: Alauddin’s Market Control vs. Tughluq’s Token Currency
Responses to Mongol (Chaghatai) invasions: Defensive vs. Proactive measures
teaching_strategy: Comparative grid analysis: Mobilization of soldiers, revenue collection, and price control
Case Study: The failure of the 'Daulatabad' shift—logistics vs. geography
Discussion on 'Bandagan' (Slaves) as a counter-weight to the old nobility
student_activities: Synthesize a debate between a Khalji administrator and a Tughluq reformer
Analyze the 'Token Currency' failure from an economic perspective (Gresham's Law basics)
assessment: Essay: 'Comparing the administrative efficacy of Alauddin Khalji and Muhammad Tughluq'
Technical definition and critique of the 'Iqta' system

lesson_plan.title: Fragmentation and the Rise of Sher Shah Sur
topics_covered: The 15th-century decline: Sayyids and Lodis
Sher Shah Sur: Administrative standardization (Currency, Land revenue, Sarai)
The Sultanate's impact on early Mughal state-building
teaching_strategy: Evaluating the 'Managerial' style of Sher Shah Sur as a precursor to Akbar
Analyzing the decentralization into regional kingdoms (Jaunpur, Bengal, Malwa)
Final concept map: The Sultanate's journey from a fortified town to a subcontinental power
student_activities: Research Sher Shah Sur’s 'Rupiya' and 'Grand Trunk Road' legacy
Complete a high-level mind map integrating politics, administration, and society
assessment: Evaluation of the 'Administrative Legacy' mind map

teaching_aids: NCERT History Textbook: Our Pasts - II
Topographical maps of Medieval Delhi and Southern India
High-res images of Sultanate inscriptions and coinage
Comparison charts for Khalji and Tughluq administrations
Primary source excerpts (Barani and Ibn Battuta)
evaluation_methods: Economic interpretation of medieval currency reforms
Map work based on military logistics
teacher_tip: At the hard level, emphasize the 'Turkish' character of the early Sultanate and how it evolved into an 'Indian' Sultanate. Contrast Muhammad Tughluq’s 'failure' with his intellectual brilliance to show the complexities of historical figures.
